name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
إِنَّآ أَنزَلْنَآ إِلَيْكَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ بِٱلْحَقِّ لِتَحْكُمَ بَيْنَ ٱلنَّاسِ بِمَآ أَرَىٰكَ ٱللَّهُ ۚ وَلَا تَكُن لِّلْخَآئِنِينَ خَصِيمًا (١٠٥)
```

```english
Indeed We have sent down to you the Book with the truth, so that you may judge between the people by what Allah has shown you; do not be an advocate for the traitors, (105)
```

```arabic
وَٱسْتَغْفِرِ ٱللَّهَ ۖ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ غَفُورًا رَّحِيمًا (١٠٦)
```

```english
and plead to Allah for forgiveness; indeed Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (106)
```

```arabic
وَلَا تُجَـٰدِلْ عَنِ ٱلَّذِينَ يَخْتَانُونَ أَنفُسَهُمْ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يُحِبُّ مَن كَانَ خَوَّانًا أَثِيمًا (١٠٧)
```

```english
And do not plead for those who betray themselves; indeed Allah does not like someone who is treacherous and sinful. (107)
```

```arabic
يَسْتَخْفُونَ مِنَ ٱلنَّاسِ وَلَا يَسْتَخْفُونَ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ وَهُوَ مَعَهُمْ إِذْ يُبَيِّتُونَ مَا لَا يَرْضَىٰ مِنَ ٱلْقَوْلِ ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ بِمَا يَعْمَلُونَ مُحِيطًا (١٠٨)
```

```english
They try to hide [their real character] from people, but they do not try to hide from Allah, though He is with them when they conspire overnight with a discourse that He does not approve of. And Allah comprehends whatever they do. (108)
```

```arabic
هَـٰٓأَنتُمْ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ جَـٰدَلْتُمْ عَنْهُمْ فِى ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا فَمَن يُجَـٰدِلُ ٱللَّهَ عَنْهُمْ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ أَم مَّن يَكُونُ عَلَيْهِمْ وَكِيلًا (١٠٩)
```

```english
Aha! There you are, pleading for them in the life of this world! But who will plead for them with Allah on the Day of Resurrection, or will be their defender? (109)
```

```arabic
وَمَن يَعْمَلْ سُوٓءًا أَوْ يَظْلِمْ نَفْسَهُۥ ثُمَّ يَسْتَغْفِرِ ٱللَّهَ يَجِدِ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورًا رَّحِيمًا (١١٠)
```

```english
Whoever commits evil or wrongs himself and then pleads to Allah for forgiveness, will find Allah all-forgiving, all-merciful. (110)
```

```arabic
وَمَن يَكْسِبْ إِثْمًا فَإِنَّمَا يَكْسِبُهُۥ عَلَىٰ نَفْسِهِۦ ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ عَلِيمًا حَكِيمًا (١١١)
```

```english
Whoever commits a sin, commits it only against himself; and Allah is all-knowing, all-wise. (111)
```

```arabic
وَمَن يَكْسِبْ خَطِيٓـَٔةً أَوْ إِثْمًا ثُمَّ يَرْمِ بِهِۦ بَرِيٓـًٔا فَقَدِ ٱحْتَمَلَ بُهْتَـٰنًا وَإِثْمًا مُّبِينًا (١١٢)
```

```english
But someone who commits an iniquity or sin and then accuses an innocent person of it, is indeed guilty of calumny and a flagrant sin. (112)
```

```arabic
وَلَوْلَا فَضْلُ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْكَ وَرَحْمَتُهُۥ لَهَمَّت طَّآئِفَةٌ مِّنْهُمْ أَن يُضِلُّوكَ وَمَا يُضِلُّونَ إِلَّآ أَنفُسَهُمْ ۖ وَمَا يَضُرُّونَكَ مِن شَىْءٍ ۚ وَأَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْكَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ وَٱلْحِكْمَةَ وَعَلَّمَكَ مَا لَمْ تَكُن تَعْلَمُ ۚ وَكَانَ فَضْلُ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْكَ عَظِيمًا (١١٣)
```

```english
Were it not for Allah’s grace and His mercy on you, a group of them were bent on leading you astray; but they do not mislead anyone except themselves, and they cannot do you any harm. Allah has sent down to you the Book and wisdom, and He has taught you what you did not know, and great is Allah’s grace upon you. (113)
```

```arabic
۞ لَّا خَيْرَ فِى كَثِيرٍ مِّن نَّجْوَىٰهُمْ إِلَّا مَنْ أَمَرَ بِصَدَقَةٍ أَوْ مَعْرُوفٍ أَوْ إِصْلَـٰحٍۭ بَيْنَ ٱلنَّاسِ ۚ وَمَن يَفْعَلْ ذَٰلِكَ ٱبْتِغَآءَ مَرْضَاتِ ٱللَّهِ فَسَوْفَ نُؤْتِيهِ أَجْرًا عَظِيمًا (١١٤)
```

```english
There is no good in much of their secret talks, excepting him who enjoins charity or what is right or reconciliation between people, and whoever does that, seeking Allah’s pleasure, soon We shall give him a great reward. (114)
```

```arabic
وَمَن يُشَاقِقِ ٱلرَّسُولَ مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا تَبَيَّنَ لَهُ ٱلْهُدَىٰ وَيَتَّبِعْ غَيْرَ سَبِيلِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ نُوَلِّهِۦ مَا تَوَلَّىٰ وَنُصْلِهِۦ جَهَنَّمَ ۖ وَسَآءَتْ مَصِيرًا (١١٥)
```

```english
Whoever defies the Apostle, after the guidance has become manifest to him, and follows a way other than that of the faithful, We shall abandon him to his devices and We shall make him enter hell, and it is an evil destination. (115)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يَغْفِرُ أَن يُشْرَكَ بِهِۦ وَيَغْفِرُ مَا دُونَ ذَٰلِكَ لِمَن يَشَآءُ ۚ وَمَن يُشْرِكْ بِٱللَّهِ فَقَدْ ضَلَّ ضَلَـٰلًۢا بَعِيدًا (١١٦)
```

```english
Indeed Allah does not forgive that any partner should be ascribed to Him, but He forgives anything besides that to whomever He wishes. And whoever ascribes partners to Allah has certainly strayed into far error. (116)
```

```arabic
إِن يَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِهِۦٓ إِلَّآ إِنَـٰثًا وَإِن يَدْعُونَ إِلَّا شَيْطَـٰنًا مَّرِيدًا (١١٧)
```

```english
They invoke none but females besides Him, and invoke none but a froward Satan, (117)
```

```arabic
لَّعَنَهُ ٱللَّهُ ۘ وَقَالَ لَأَتَّخِذَنَّ مِنْ عِبَادِكَ نَصِيبًا مَّفْرُوضًا (١١٨)
```

```english
whom Allah has cursed, and who said, ‘I will surely take of Your servants a settled share, (118)
```

```arabic
وَلَأُضِلَّنَّهُمْ وَلَأُمَنِّيَنَّهُمْ وَلَـَٔامُرَنَّهُمْ فَلَيُبَتِّكُنَّ ءَاذَانَ ٱلْأَنْعَـٰمِ وَلَـَٔامُرَنَّهُمْ فَلَيُغَيِّرُنَّ خَلْقَ ٱللَّهِ ۚ وَمَن يَتَّخِذِ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنَ وَلِيًّا مِّن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ فَقَدْ خَسِرَ خُسْرَانًا مُّبِينًا (١١٩)
```

```english
and I will lead them astray and give them [false] hopes, and prompt them to slit the ears of cattle, and I will prompt them to alter Allah’s creation.’ Whoever takes Satan as a guardian instead of Allah has certainly incurred a manifest loss. (119)
```

```arabic
يَعِدُهُمْ وَيُمَنِّيهِمْ ۖ وَمَا يَعِدُهُمُ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ إِلَّا غُرُورًا (١٢٠)
```

```english
He makes them promises and gives them [false] hopes, yet Satan does not promise them anything but delusion. (120)
```

```arabic
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ مَأْوَىٰهُمْ جَهَنَّمُ وَلَا يَجِدُونَ عَنْهَا مَحِيصًا (١٢١)
```

```english
The refuge of such shall be hell, and they will not find any escape from it. (121)
```

```arabic
وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ سَنُدْخِلُهُمْ جَنَّـٰتٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَآ أَبَدًا ۖ وَعْدَ ٱللَّهِ حَقًّا ۚ وَمَنْ أَصْدَقُ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ قِيلًا (١٢٢)
```

```english
But those who have faith and do righteous deeds, We will admit them into gardens with streams running in them, to remain in them forever—a true promise of Allah, and who is truer in speech than Allah? (122)
```

```arabic
لَّيْسَ بِأَمَانِيِّكُمْ وَلَآ أَمَانِىِّ أَهْلِ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ ۗ مَن يَعْمَلْ سُوٓءًا يُجْزَ بِهِۦ وَلَا يَجِدْ لَهُۥ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ وَلِيًّا وَلَا نَصِيرًا (١٢٣)
```

```english
It will be neither after your hopes nor the hopes of the People of the Book: whoever commits evil shall be requited for it, and he will not find for himself any guardian or helper besides Allah. (123)
```

```arabic
وَمَن يَعْمَلْ مِنَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ مِن ذَكَرٍ أَوْ أُنثَىٰ وَهُوَ مُؤْمِنٌ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ يَدْخُلُونَ ٱلْجَنَّةَ وَلَا يُظْلَمُونَ نَقِيرًا (١٢٤)
```

```english
And whoever does righteous deeds, whether male or female, should he be faithful—such shall enter paradise and they will not be wronged [so much as] the speck on a date-stone. (124)
```

```arabic
وَمَنْ أَحْسَنُ دِينًا مِّمَّنْ أَسْلَمَ وَجْهَهُۥ لِلَّهِ وَهُوَ مُحْسِنٌ وَٱتَّبَعَ مِلَّةَ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ حَنِيفًا ۗ وَٱتَّخَذَ ٱللَّهُ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ خَلِيلًا (١٢٥)
```

```english
Who has a better religion than him who submits his will to Allah, being virtuous, and follows the creed of Abraham, a Hanif? And Allah took Abraham for a dedicated friend. (125)
```

```arabic
وَلِلَّهِ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ مُّحِيطًا (١٢٦)
```

```english
To Allah belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth, and Allah comprehends all things. (126)
```

## (Commentary)

What is gained from contemplating these verses is that they possess a single context, concerned with the exhortation to justice in adjudication (القضاء) and the prohibition against a judge inclining in his judgment, or a ruler in his ruling, toward those in the wrong (المبطلين), or acting unjustly against those in the right (المحقين), whoever they may be.

This is done by referring to certain incidents that occurred at the time of the revelation of the verses, followed by an inquiry into the religious truths related to that and the command to adhere to and observe them. It serves as a reminder to the believers that religion is a reality (حقيقة), not merely a name (اسم), and that what benefits is being truly characterized by it (التلبس به), not merely being titled by it (التسمي).

It appears that this story is what is referred to by His saying, the Exalted: **وَمَنْ يَكْسِبْ خَطِيئَةً أَوْ إِثْماً ثُمَّ يَرْمِ بِهِ بَرِيئاً فَقَدِ احْتَمَلَ بُهْتاناً وَإِثْماً مُبِيناً** "But whoever commits a sin or a crime, and then blames it on an innocent person, has certainly shouldered a calumny and a flagrant sin." (4:112). This indicates that there was some form of disobedience (المعاصي) that could be attributed to another, such as theft, murder, destruction of property, or causing harm, and that it was expected that they would attempt to lead the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله astray in his judgment, while Allah is his protector.

It also appears that this story is what the initial verses refer to, as in His saying, the Exalted: **وَلا تَكُنْ لِلْخائِنِينَ خَصِيماً** "and do not be an advocate for the treacherous" (4:105), and His saying: **يَسْتَخْفُونَ مِنَ النَّاسِ** "They hide from people" (4:108), and His saying: **ها أَنْتُمْ هؤُلاءِ جادَلْتُمْ عَنْهُمْ** "Aha! There you are, pleading for them" (4:109). For although treachery (الخيانة) literally appears to refer to what occurs regarding deposits and trusts, the context of His saying: **إِنَّ اللهَ لا يُحِبُّ مَنْ كانَ خَوَّاناً أَثِيماً يَسْتَخْفُونَ مِنَ النَّاسِ** "Indeed Allah does not love one who is a treacherous sinner. They hide from people" (4:107-108)—as will be explained—suggests that what is meant is that which is realized in theft and the like. This is based on the consideration that the believers are as a single soul; the wealth belonging to some is the responsibility of others in terms of respecting its sanctity and being concerned with its preservation and protection. Thus, the transgression of some against the wealth of others is a betrayal (خيانة) of themselves.

Contemplation suggests that the story was likely a theft committed by one of them, after which the matter was brought to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله. The thief then cast the blame upon someone else who was innocent. The thief's people then pressured him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله to judge in their favor and went to great lengths to turn him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله against the innocent accused. Consequently, the verses were revealed, and Allah cleared him of what they said.

Thus, the verses apply most strongly to what has been narrated regarding the occasion of revelation (شأن النزول) concerning the story of the theft by Abu Tu‘mah ibn al-Abayriq, even if the occasions of revelation—as you have heard repeatedly—are mostly of the nature of applying traditional stories to the Quranic verses that suit them.

From these verses, one can derive the authority (حجية) of his صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله judgment, his impeccability (العصمة) — *the divine protection from sin and error* — and other truths that will be explained, Allah the Exalted willing.

His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّا أَنْزَلْنا إِلَيْكَ الْكِتابَ بِالْحَقِّ لِتَحْكُمَ بَيْنَ النَّاسِ بِما أَراكَ اللهُ** "We have sent down the Book to you with the truth, so that you may judge between the people by what Allah has shown you." (4:105). The apparent meaning of "judging between the people" is to adjudicate between them in their disputes and litigations regarding judicial matters and to resolve differences through a ruling. Allah the Exalted has made judging between people the purpose of sending down the Book. Thus, the content of the verse aligns with what is contained in His saying, the Exalted: **كانَ النَّاسُ أُمَّةً واحِدَةً فَبَعَثَ اللهُ النَّبِيِّينَ مُبَشِّرِينَ وَمُنْذِرِينَ وَأَنْزَلَ مَعَهُمُ الْكِتابَ بِالْحَقِّ لِيَحْكُمَ بَيْنَ النَّاسِ فِيمَا اخْتَلَفُوا فِيهِ** "Mankind were a single community; then Allah sent the prophets as bearers of good news and as warners, and He sent down with them the Book with the truth, that it may judge between the people concerning that about which they differed." (2:213). A detailed discussion on this has already passed.

This verse, **إِنَّا أَنْزَلْنا إِلَيْكَ الْكِتابَ** (etc.), is similar in its specific context to that verse, **كانَ النَّاسُ أُمَّةً واحِدَةً**, in its generality. However, it adds to it by indicating the establishment of the right of judgment for the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and the authority (الحجية) of his opinion and view. For judgment (الحكم), which is the decisive resolution in adjudication and the settling of disputes, is inseparable from the exercise of the judge's view and the expression of his conviction, in addition to the knowledge he possesses of general rulings and universal laws (القوانين الكلية) in cases of litigation. Knowledge of universal rulings and the rights of people is one thing, while the decisive resolution and judgment that the specific case of dispute corresponds to one ruling rather than another is something else.

Therefore, what is meant by "showing" (الإراءة) in His saying: **لِتَحْكُمَ بَيْنَ النَّاسِ بِما أَراكَ اللهُ** is the creation of the view and the making known of the judgment, not the teaching of rulings and laws (الشرائع), as some have conjectured.

The content of the verse, according to what the context provides, is that Allah sent down the Book to you and taught you its rulings, laws, and wisdom so that you may add to them the view He has created for you and the judgment He has made known to you, thereby judging between the people and resolving their differences.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَلا تَكُنْ لِلْخائِنِينَ خَصِيماً** "and do not be an advocate for the treacherous" (4:105). This is conjoined to the preceding declarative sentence because it carries the meaning of a command (الإنشاء), as if it were said: "So judge between them and do not be an advocate for the treacherous."

The **advocate** (الخصيم) is the one who defends a claim or what is in its position. In this is a prohibition for him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله from being an advocate for the treacherous against those who demand their rights from them, thereby defending the treacherous and nullifying the rights of those who are in the right among the claimants.

It might be possible to derive from the conjunction of His saying: **وَلا تَكُنْ لِلْخائِنِينَ** to what preceded it—which is His absolute command to him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله to judge—that what is meant by treachery (الخيانة) is absolute transgression against the rights of others by those from whom such behavior is unbecoming, not specifically the betrayal of trusts. Although a specific term is sometimes conjoined to a general one for a particular concern regarding its status, the context here seems devoid of such specific concern. A completion of this discussion will follow.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَاسْتَغْفِرِ اللهَ إِنَّ اللهَ كانَ غَفُوراً رَحِيماً** "And plead to Allah for forgiveness; indeed Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful." (4:106). It appears that seeking forgiveness (الاستغفار) here is to ask Allah, the Glorified, to veil (الستر) what is in human nature of the possibility of encroaching upon rights and inclining toward desire (الهوى), and to forgive that. It has been mentioned repeatedly that pardon (العفو) and forgiveness (المغفرة) are used in His speech in various contexts, all unified by the concept of sin (الذنب) — *which is a distancing from the truth in some way*. The meaning—and Allah knows best—is: Do not be an advocate for the treacherous nor incline toward them, and ask Allah, the Glorified, to grant you success in that and to veil your soul from inclining toward defending their treachery and from the caprice of the soul (هوى النفس) gaining mastery over you.

The evidence for this intended meaning is what is found at the end of these noble verses: **وَلَوْ لا فَضْلُ اللهِ عَلَيْكَ وَرَحْمَتُهُ لَهَمَّتْ طائِفَةٌ مِنْهُمْ أَنْ يُضِلُّوكَ وَما يُضِلُّونَ إِلَّا أَنْفُسَهُمْ وَما يَضُرُّونَكَ مِنْ شَيْءٍ** "Were it not for Allah’s grace and His mercy on you, a group of them were bent on leading you astray; but they do not mislead anyone except themselves, and they cannot do you any harm." (4:113). For the verse explicitly states that they do not harm the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله even if they exert their utmost effort in stirring his emotions to favor falsehood and make it prevail over the truth. Thus, the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله is in a state of divine security from harm, and Allah protects him (يعصمه); so he does not act unjustly in his judgment, nor does he incline toward injustice, nor does he follow desire. Among injustice and the blameworthy inclination toward desire is to differentiate in one's judgment between the strong and the weak, or a friend and an enemy, or a believer and a protected disbeliever (كافر ذمي), or a relative and a stranger. Thus, the command for him to seek forgiveness is not due to the commission of a sin carrying a burden or consequence, nor because he was on the verge of something unpraiseworthy; rather, it is to ask Allah to make him prevail over the caprice of the soul. There is no doubt of his need for his Lord in this and his lack of independence from Him, even if he possesses impeccability (عصمة), for Allah, the Glorified, does as He wills.

This impeccability (العصمة) operates within the sphere of what is considered obedience (طاعة) and disobedience (معصية), and actions for which one is praised or blamed, not within the sphere of external reality (الواقع الخارجي). In other words, the verses indicate that he صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله is secure from following desire and inclining toward falsehood. As for whether the judgment he passes based on the prescribed rules and the apparent laws of adjudication—such as his saying: **البينة على المدعي واليمين على من أنكر** "The burden of proof is on the claimant and the oath is upon the one who denies"—and the like, always coincides with what is the truth in reality (في الواقع), thereby always resulting in the victory of the one in the right and the defeat of the one in the wrong in their claim; the verses do not indicate this at all. Nor are the apparent laws capable of leading to that with certainty, for they are signs (أمارات) that distinguish between truth and falsehood mostly (غالباً), not always. It is obvious that the "mostly" does not necessitate the "always."

From what has preceded, the flaw in the words of some commentators becomes apparent where they mentioned regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَاسْتَغْفِرِ اللهَ**, that it is a command to seek forgiveness for what the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله intended of defending and protecting this aforementioned traitor in the verse, after his people had asked him to defend him and be an advocate for him against a Jew.

This is because even this much would be a blameworthy effect from them, and Allah, the Glorified, has negated all harm from him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَلا تُجادِلْ عَنِ الَّذِينَ يَخْتانُونَ أَنْفُسَهُمْ** "And do not plead for those who deceive themselves" (4:107). It is said that the attribution of treachery (الخيانة) to the soul is because its consequence returns to it, or that every disobedience is considered a betrayal of the soul, just as it is considered an injustice (ظلم) against it, as He, the Exalted, said: **عَلِمَ اللهُ أَنَّكُمْ كُنْتُمْ تَخْتانُونَ أَنْفُسَكُمْ** "Allah knew that you used to betray yourselves" (2:187).

It is possible to derive from the verse, with the aid of what the Quran indicates—that the believers are as a single soul, and that the wealth of one of them is the wealth of all of them, which all must preserve and protect from loss and destruction—that the transgression of some against others through theft and the like is a betrayal (اختياناً) of themselves.

In His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّ اللهَ لا يُحِبُّ مَنْ كانَ خَوَّاناً أَثِيماً** "Indeed Allah does not love one who is a treacherous sinner" (4:107), there is an indication of the persistence of these traitors in their treachery. This is reinforced by His saying: **أَثِيماً** (sinner), for *athīm* is more emphatic in meaning than *ithm* (sin), being an intensive adjective (صفة مشبهة) indicating stability. Furthermore, His saying: **يَخْتانُونَ أَنْفُسَهُمْ** (they deceive themselves) is not devoid of an indication of persistence, as is His saying: **لِلْخائِنِينَ** (for the treacherous), where He expressed it with a descriptive noun (الوصف) rather than saying something like "for those who betrayed," as He did in His saying: **فَقَدْ خانُوا اللهَ مِنْ قَبْلُ فَأَمْكَنَ مِنْهُمْ** "they have already been treacherous to Allah before, and He gave [you] mastery over them" (8:71).

From these clues and their like, it appears that the meaning of the verse—with respect to the occasion of revelation—is: Do not be an advocate for these people, nor plead for them, for they are persistent in treachery, excessive in it, and established in sin, and Allah does not love one who is a treacherous sinner. This supports what has been narrated in the occasions of revelation regarding the verses being revealed about Abu Tu‘mah ibn al-Abayriq, as will follow.

The meaning of the verse—disregarding the specific incident—is: Do not defend in your judgment those who are persistent in treachery and continue in it, for Allah does not love the treacherous sinner. Just as He, the Exalted, does not love much treachery, He does not love a little of it. If it were possible for Him to love a little of it, it would be possible for Him to love much of it. Since this is the case, Allah prohibits defending a little treachery just as He prohibits defending much of it. As for one who was treacherous in one matter but then disputes in another matter while being in the right in his dispute, defending him is a defense that is neither prohibited nor forbidden, nor is it prohibited by His saying: **وَلا تَكُنْ لِلْخائِنِينَ خَصِيماً** (etc.).

His saying, the Exalted: **يَسْتَخْفُونَ مِنَ النَّاسِ وَلا يَسْتَخْفُونَ مِنَ اللهِ** "They hide from people, but they do not hide from Allah" (4:108). This is also among the evidences for what we previously stated: that the verses (105–126) all possess a single context, revealed concerning a single story, which is the one referred to by His saying: **وَمَنْ يَكْسِبْ خَطِيئَةً أَوْ إِثْماً ثُمَّ يَرْمِ بِهِ بَرِيئاً** (etc.). This is because hiding (الاستخفاء) is only suitable for actions that can be blamed on others, such as theft and the like. Thus, it is reinforced that what this verse and the preceding verses refer to is the same as what is referred to by His saying: **وَمَنْ يَكْسِبْ خَطِيئَةً أَوْ إِثْماً ثُمَّ يَرْمِ بِهِ** (etc.).

Hiding from Allah is an impossible matter, as nothing is hidden from Allah in the earth or in the heaven. Therefore, its opposite—namely, not hiding—is also a compelled matter beyond one's power. If it were beyond power, it would not be subject to blame or reproach, as is the apparent sense of the verse. However, it appears that "hiding" is a metaphor (كناية) for "feeling shame" (الاستحياء). For this reason, He qualified His saying: **وَلا يَسْتَخْفُونَ مِنَ اللهِ** (firstly) with His saying: **وَهُوَ مَعَهُمْ إِذْ يُبَيِّتُونَ ما لا يَرْضى مِنَ الْقَوْلِ** "while He is with them when they plot at night with words that do not please Him" (4:108). This indicates that they were devising a ruse by night to clear themselves of this blameworthy treachery, plotting in that a speech with which Allah, the Glorified, is not pleased. Then He qualified it (secondly) with His saying: **وَكانَ اللهُ بِما يَعْمَلُونَ مُحِيطاً** "and Allah encompasses what they do" (4:108), indicating His encompassing them in all states, including the state of the crime they committed. The qualification with these two constraints—namely, His saying: **وَهُوَ مَعَهُمْ** and His saying: **وَكانَ اللهُ**—is a qualification by the general after the specific. It is, in reality, a justification for their lack of shame before Allah by a specific cause and then by a general one.

His saying, the Exalted: **ها أَنْتُمْ هؤُلاءِ جادَلْتُمْ عَنْهُمْ فِي الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا** "Aha! There you are, pleading for them in the life of this world" (4:109). This is an explanation of the futility of pleading for them, and that they do not benefit from it, presented in the form of an interrogative. The meaning is that if pleading for them were to benefit them, it would only benefit them in the life of this world, which has no value before Allah. As for the afterlife, which has great value before Allah, or the occasion for defense on the Day of Resurrection, there will be no defender there for the treacherous, nor any to plead for them; rather, they will have no trustee (وكيل) on that day to undertake the management of their affairs or the rectification of their conditions.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَمَنْ يَعْمَلْ سُوءاً أَوْ يَظْلِمْ نَفْسَهُ** "Whoever commits an evil or wrongs himself" (4:110). In this is an encouragement and urging for those traitors to return to their Lord by seeking forgiveness. It appears that the alternative between "evil" (السوء) and "wronging oneself" (ظلم النفس), and the progression from evil to wronging, is because what is meant by evil is transgression against others, and by wronging is transgression against oneself. Or, it may be that evil is lesser than wronging, like a small disobedience compared to a large one; and Allah knows best.

This verse and the two verses after it are all speech directed toward a single purpose: the explanation of the matter of sin (الإثم) that a person earns by his action. Each of the three verses undertakes the explanation of one of its aspects. The first verse explains that the disobedience a person commits, whose consequence affects his soul and is recorded in the book of his deeds, is something for which the servant may repent to Allah and seek His forgiveness; if he does so, he will find Allah all-forgiving, all-merciful.

The second verse reminds man that the sin he earns, he earns only against himself, and it is not something that can bypass him and attach to another through blaming or fabrication and the like.

The third verse clarifies that the fault (الخطيئة) or sin (الإثم) that a person earns, if he blames it on an innocent person, that blaming is another sin beyond the original fault or sin.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَمَنْ يَكْسِبْ إِثْماً فَإِنَّما يَكْسِبُهُ عَلى نَفْسِهِ وَكانَ اللهُ عَلِيماً حَكِيماً** "Whoever earns a sin, earns it only against himself; and Allah is all-knowing, all-wise." (4:111). It has already been mentioned that the verse is linked in content to the following verse dealing with blaming others for a fault or sin; thus, this one is like a premise for that one. Accordingly, His saying: **فَإِنَّما يَكْسِبُهُ عَلى نَفْسِهِ** is directed toward a restrictive designation (قصر التعيين). In the verse is an admonition for whoever earns a sin and then blames it on an innocent person. The meaning—and Allah knows best—is: It is incumbent upon whoever earns a sin to remember that the sin he earns, he earns only against himself, not against another; and that he is the one who did it, not anyone else, even if he blames it on another or if another undertakes to carry his sin for him. Allah is all-knowing (عليماً), knowing that this earner did it, and that he is the one who did it, not the innocent person blamed for it; and He is all-wise (حكيماً), not punishing for a sin except its sinner, nor for a burden except its bearer, as He, the Exalted, said: **لَهَا مَا كَسَبَتْ وَعَلَيْهَا مَا اكْتَسَبَتْ** "It shall have what it has earned, and it shall bear [the responsibility of] what it has perpetrated" (2:286), and He said: **وَلا تَزِرُ وازِرَةٌ وِزْرَ أُخْرى** "no bearer shall bear another’s burden" (6:164), and He said: **وَقالَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اتَّبِعُوا سَبِيلَنا وَلْنَحْمِلْ خَطاياكُمْ وَما هُمْ بِحامِلِينَ مِنْ خَطاياهُمْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ إِنَّهُمْ لَكاذِبُونَ** "The faithless say to the faithful, ‘Follow our way and we will bear your sins.’ But they will not bear any of their sins. They are indeed liars." (29:12).

His saying, the Exalted: **وَمَنْ يَكْسِبْ خَطِيئَةً أَوْ إِثْماً ثُمَّ يَرْمِ بِهِ بَرِيئاً فَقَدِ احْتَمَلَ بُهْتاناً وَإِثْماً مُبِيناً** "But whoever commits a sin or a crime, and then blames it on an innocent person, has certainly shouldered a calumny and a flagrant sin." (4:112). Al-Raghib said in *al-Mufradat*: "If someone intends one thing but another happens from him, it is said: he erred (أخطأ). If it happens as he intended, it is said: he was correct (أصاب). It may also be said of one who does an act that is not good or intends an intention that is not beautiful: he erred. For this reason, it is said: he hit the error, and he missed the right, and he hit the right, and he missed the error. This word is homonymous, as you see, fluctuating between meanings that one who seeks truths must contemplate."

He said: "The fault (الخطيئة) and the evil deed (السيئة) are close, but 'fault' is most often said of that which is not intended in itself, but rather the intention is a cause for the generation of that act from him, such as one who shoots at game but hits a person, or drinks an intoxicant and commits a crime in his intoxication. The cause is of two types: a prohibited cause, such as drinking an intoxicant, where the error generated from it is not excused; and a non-prohibited cause, such as shooting at game. He, the Exalted, said: **لَيْسَ عَلَيْكُمْ جُناحٌ فِيما أَخْطَأْتُمْ بِهِ وَلكِنْ ما تَعَمَّدَتْ قُلُوبُكُمْ** 'There is no sin upon you in what you may mistake therein, but [only] in what your hearts intend' (33:5). And He, the Exalted, said: **وَمَنْ يَكْسِبْ خَطِيئَةً أَوْ إِثْماً**, so the 'fault' here is that which is not from an intention to do it." (End of quote).

I think that "fault" (الخطيئة) is among the descriptions that have become independent of their nouns due to frequent use, like "calamity" (المصيبة), "misfortune" (الرزية), and "disposition" (السليقة). The weight *fa‘īlah* indicates the storage and settlement of the event. Thus, the fault is the action in which error (الخطأ) has been stored and settled. Error is the occurring act that a person does not intend, such as accidental killing; this is its original meaning. Then it was expanded to include what a person ought not to intend if his soul were in its primordial soundness (سلامتها الفطرة). Thus, every disobedience and effect of disobedience is among the instances of error according to this expansion. The fault is the action or the effect of the action that a person did not intend (and is not then considered a disobedience) or ought not to have intended (and is then considered a disobedience or the consequence of a disobedience).

However, since Allah, the Glorified, attributed it to earning (الكسب) in His saying: **وَمَنْ يَكْسِبْ خَطِيئَةً**, what is meant by it is the fault that is a disobedience. Thus, the fault in the verse is that which arises from an intention to do it, even if it is of a nature that it ought not to be intended.

It has already passed regarding His saying, the Exalted: **قُلْ فِيهِما إِثْمٌ** "Say, ‘There is a great sin in both of them’" (2:219) that **sin** (الإثم) is the action that, by its consequence, causes the deprivation of many good things for man, such as drinking wine, gambling, and theft, which prevent man from acquiring the vital goods of life and cause a social decline that reduces his social weight and strips him of general trust and confidence.

Accordingly, the combination of "fault" and "sin" in the form of an alternative and the attribution of both to earning in His saying: **وَمَنْ يَكْسِبْ خَطِيئَةً أَوْ إِثْماً** (etc.) necessitates the specialization of each with its own meaning. The meaning—and Allah knows best—is: Whoever earns a disobedience whose consequence does not exceed its specific instance, such as neglecting some obligations like fasting or committing some prohibitions like eating blood; or earns a disobedience whose consequence persists, such as killing a soul without right or theft, and then blames it on an innocent person by attributing it to him, has certainly shouldered a calumny and a flagrant sin.

In naming the attribution of an evil deed to another "blaming" (الرمي) — *literally "throwing," used for arrows* — and likewise in the use of "shouldering" (الاحتمال) for accepting the burden of the sin of calumny, there is a subtle metaphor (استعارة). It is as if the fabricator strikes the innocent accused by shooting him with an arrow, and his strike causes him to carry a burden that occupies him from every good for the rest of his life without ever leaving him.

From what has preceded, the reason for the variation in the expression for disobedience in the noble verses becomes clear—sometimes as sin (إثم), and other times as fault (خطيئة), evil (سوء), wronging (ظلم), treachery (خيانة), and misguidance (ضلال). Each of these words is suitable in its meaning for the place it occupies.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَوْ لا فَضْلُ اللهِ عَلَيْكَ وَرَحْمَتُهُ لَهَمَّتْ** (to the end of the verse). The context indicates that what is meant by their being bent on leading the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله astray is their being bent on making him agree to defend those whom Allah, the Exalted, named at the beginning of the verses as the treacherous, and to plead for them. Accordingly, what is meant by this "group" (طائفة) are also those whom Allah, the Glorified, turned to address with His saying: **ها أَنْتُمْ هؤُلاءِ جادَلْتُمْ عَنْهُمْ فِي الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا** (etc.), and it applies to the people of Abu Tu‘mah according to what will follow.

As for His saying: **وَما يُضِلُّونَ إِلَّا أَنْفُسَهُمْ** "but they do not mislead anyone except themselves," what is meant by it, by the clue of His saying after it: **وَما يَضُرُّونَكَ مِنْ شَيْءٍ** "and they cannot do you any harm," is that the misguidance of these people does not exceed themselves nor does it pass from them to you. They are the misguided ones by what they intended, because it is a disobedience, and every disobedience is misguidance (ضلال).

This speech has another meaning that was previously alluded to in the discussion on His saying: **وَما يُضِلُّونَ إِلَّا أَنْفُسَهُمْ وَما يَشْعُرُونَ** "but they do not mislead anyone except themselves, and they are not aware" (3:69) in the third volume of this book, but it is not suitable for this context.

As for His saying: **وَما يَضُرُّونَكَ مِنْ شَيْءٍ وَأَنْزَلَ اللهُ عَلَيْكَ** "and they cannot do you any harm, and Allah has sent down to you," it contains an absolute negation of their harming the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله. However, the apparent context is that it is qualified by His saying: **وَأَنْزَلَ اللهُ عَلَيْكَ الْكِتابَ**, considering it a circumstantial clause (جملة حالية) referring to the pronoun in His saying: **يَضُرُّونَكَ**, even if most often a verbal sentence starting with a past tense verb is accompanied by *qad*, as mentioned by the grammarians. Accordingly, the speech is directed toward negating people's harming the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله absolutely, whether in knowledge or action.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَأَنْزَلَ اللهُ عَلَيْكَ الْكِتابَ وَالْحِكْمَةَ وَعَلَّمَكَ ما لَمْ تَكُنْ تَعْلَمُ** "and Allah has sent down to you the Book and wisdom, and He has taught you what you did not know" (4:113). The apparent meaning of the speech, as we alluded, is that it is in the position of providing the reason for His saying: **وَما يَضُرُّونَكَ مِنْ شَيْءٍ**, or for the whole of His saying: **وَما يُضِلُّونَ إِلَّا أَنْفُسَهُمْ وَما يَضُرُّونَكَ مِنْ شَيْءٍ**. In any case, this sending down and teaching is what prevents their effect in leading him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله astray; thus, it is the criterion for his impeccability (عصمته).

## (A Discussion on the Meaning of Impeccability)

The apparent sense of the verse is that the matter by which impeccability (العصمة) is realized is a type of knowledge (علم) that prevents its possessor from being characterized by disobedience and error. In other words, it is a knowledge that prevents misguidance (ضلال), just as other moral traits such as courage, chastity, and generosity are each a firmly rooted mental image (صورة علمية) necessitating the realization of their effects and preventing the characterization by their opposites, such as the effects of cowardice, recklessness, apathy, greed, miserliness, and extravagance.

Beneficial knowledge and profound wisdom (الحكمة), although they necessitate the purification of their possessor from falling into the perils of vices and being stained by the filth of disobediences—as we observe in men of knowledge, wisdom, and the virtuous among the people of godwariness (تقوى) and religion—nevertheless, this is a predominant cause (سبب غالبي) like all other causes existing in this natural, material world. You will hardly find one characterized by a perfection whose perfection prevents him from deficiencies and protects him from error with a permanent protection without fail; this is a prevailing norm in all the causes we see and observe.

The reason for this is that the various conscious powers (القوى الشعورية) in man cause some of them to be oblivious to the ruling of others or to have a weakened attention toward them. For instance, the possessor of the faculty of godwariness, as long as he is conscious of the virtue of his godwariness, does not incline toward following an unpleasing desire and acts according to the requirement of his godwariness. However, the ignition of the fire of desire and the attraction of his soul to this type of consciousness may veil him from remembering the virtue of godwariness, or the consciousness of godwariness may weaken; thus, he soon commits what godwariness does not approve of and chooses the baseness of greed. In this manner are all other conscious causes in man; otherwise, man would not deviate from the ruling of any of these causes as long as the cause remains standing and there is no obstacle preventing its effect. Thus, all these failures are attributed to the mutual conflict between godwariness and other causes, and the prevailing of some over others.

From here it becomes clear that this power called the power of impeccability (قوة العصمة) is a conscious, cognitive cause (سبب شعوري علمي) that is never overcome. If it were of the nature of the types of consciousness and perception we are familiar with, failure would seep into it, and it would occasionally falter in its effect. Therefore, this knowledge is not of the same genus as other familiar knowledges and perceptions that accept acquisition and learning.

Allah the Exalted has alluded to it in the address He specified for His Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله by His saying: **وَأَنْزَلَ اللهُ عَلَيْكَ الْكِتابَ وَالْحِكْمَةَ وَعَلَّمَكَ ما لَمْ تَكُنْ تَعْلَمُ**. This is a specific address that we do not truly comprehend, as we have no taste for this type of knowledge and consciousness. However, what appears to us somewhat from His other speech—such as His saying: **قُلْ مَنْ كانَ عَدُوًّا لِجِبْرِيلَ فَإِنَّهُ نَزَّلَهُ عَلى قَلْبِكَ** "Say, ‘Whoever is an enemy of Gabriel [should know that] it is he who has brought it down on your heart’" (2:97), and His saying: **نَزَلَ بِهِ الرُّوحُ الْأَمِينُ عَلى قَلْبِكَ لِتَكُونَ مِنَ الْمُنْذِرِينَ بِلِسانٍ عَرَبِيٍّ مُبِينٍ** "The Trustworthy Spirit has brought it down to your heart, so that you may be one of the warners, in a clear Arabic language" (26:193-195)—is that the aforementioned "sending down" (الإنزال) is of the genus of knowledge. It appears from another perspective that this is of the nature of revelation (الوحي) and speaking (التكليم), as appears from His saying: **شَرَعَ لَكُمْ مِنَ الدِّينِ ما وَصَّى بِهِ نُوحاً وَالَّذِي أَوْحَيْنا إِلَيْكَ وَما وَصَّيْنا بِهِ إِبْراهِيمَ وَمُوسى وَعِيسى** "He has prescribed for you the religion which He had enjoined upon Noah and which We have revealed to you, and which We had enjoined upon Abraham, Moses and Jesus" (42:13), and His saying: **إِنَّا أَوْحَيْنا إِلَيْكَ كَما أَوْحَيْنا إِلى نُوحٍ وَالنَّبِيِّينَ مِنْ بَعْدِهِ** "We have indeed revealed to you as We revealed to Noah and the prophets after him" (4:163), and His saying: **إِنْ أَتَّبِعُ إِلَّا ما يُوحى إِلَيَّ** "I only follow what is revealed to me" (6:50), and His saying: **إِنَّما أَتَّبِعُ ما يُوحى إِلَيَّ** "I only follow what is revealed to me" (7:203).

It is gained from the verses in their variety that what is meant by "sending down" is revelation—the revelation of the Book and wisdom. This is a type of divine teaching for His Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله. However, that which He refers to by His saying: **وَعَلَّمَكَ ما لَمْ تَكُنْ تَعْلَمُ** is not only what He taught him through the revelation of the Book and wisdom. For the context of the verse is the Prophet's صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله judgment in occurring incidents and the claims brought before him based on his own view; this is not part of the Book and wisdom, even if it depends on them; rather, it is his own view and perspective.

From here it becomes clear that what is meant by "sending down" and "teaching" in His saying: **وَأَنْزَلَ اللهُ عَلَيْكَ الْكِتابَ وَالْحِكْمَةَ وَعَلَّمَكَ ما لَمْ تَكُنْ تَعْلَمُ** are two types of knowledge: one is teaching through revelation and the descent of the Trustworthy Spirit upon the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, and the other is teaching through a type of casting into the heart and subtle divine inspiration (الإلهام) without the descent of the angel. This is what is supported by the narrations regarding the knowledge of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله.

Accordingly, the meaning of His saying: **وَعَلَّمَكَ ما لَمْ تَكُنْ تَعْلَمُ** is: He gave you a type of knowledge that, had He not given it to you from His presence, the ordinary causes by which man learns what he acquires of sciences would not have sufficed to give it to you.

It has become clear from all we have presented that this divine gift which we call the power of impeccability (قوة العصمة) is a type of knowledge and consciousness that differs from all other types of knowledge in that it is never overcome by any of the conscious powers; rather, it is the one that prevails and dominates over them, employing them. For this reason, it protects its possessor from misguidance and error absolutely. It has been mentioned in narrations that the Prophet and the Imam possess a spirit called the Holy Spirit (روح القدس) which supports them and protects them from disobedience and error. This is what is referred to by His saying, the Exalted: **وَكَذلِكَ أَوْحَيْنا إِلَيْكَ رروحاً مِنْ أَمْرِنا ما كُنْتَ تَدْرِي مَا الْكِتابُ وَلَا الْإِيمانُ وَلكِنْ جَعَلْناهُ نُوراً نَهْدِي بِهِ مَنْ نَشاءُ مِنْ عِبادِنا** "Accordingly We have revealed to you a spirit of Our command. You did not know what the Book is, nor what is faith; but We made it a light by which We guide whomever We wish of Our servants" (42:52), by taking the verse according to its apparent sense of casting the word of the Spirit, the teacher and guide, to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله. Similar to it is His saying, the Exalted: **وَجَعَلْناهُمْ أَئِمَّةً يَهْدُونَ بِأَمْرِنا وَأَوْحَيْنا إِلَيْهِمْ فِعْلَ الْخَيْراتِ وَإِقامَ الصَّلاةِ وَإِيتاءَ الزَّكاةِ وَكانُوا لَنا عابِدِينَ** "We made them imams, guiding by Our command, and We revealed to them the performance of good deeds, the maintenance of prayer, and the giving of zakat, and they used to be Our worshippers" (21:73), based on what will follow in the explanation of the verse's meaning—Allah the Mighty willing—that what is meant is the Holy Spirit's supporting the Imam in performing good deeds and worshipping Allah, the Glorified.

It has also become clear from what has passed that what is meant by "the Book" in His saying: **وَأَنْزَلَ اللهُ عَلَيْكَ الْكِتابَ وَالْحِكْمَةَ وَعَلَّمَكَ ما لَمْ تَكُنْ تَعْلَمُ** is the revelation sent down to resolve the differences of people, according to His saying, the Exalted: **كانَ النَّاسُ أُمَّةً واحِدَةً فَبَعَثَ اللهُ النَّبِيِّينَ مُبَشِّرِينَ وَمُنْذِرِينَ وَأَنْزَلَ مَعَهُمُ الْكِتابَ بِالْحَقِّ لِيَحْكُمَ بَيْنَ النَّاسِ فِيمَا اخْتَلَفُوا فِيهِ** (2:213), which was explained in the second volume of this book.

What is meant by "wisdom" (الحكمة) is all other divine gnoses (المعارف الإلهية) sent down through revelation, beneficial for this world and the hereafter. What is meant by His saying: **وَعَلَّمَكَ ما لَمْ تَكُنْ تَعْلَمُ** is other than the universal, general gnoses from the Book and wisdom.

By this, the flaw in the words of some commentators in interpreting the verse becomes apparent. Some interpreted "the Book" as the Quran, "wisdom" as the rulings within it, and "what you did not know" as the rulings and the Unseen (الغيب). Others interpreted "the Book and wisdom" as the Quran and the Sunnah, and "what you did not know" as the laws and the reports of the earlier messengers and other sciences, among other things they mentioned. The reason for their weakness has become clear from what has passed, so we shall not repeat it.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَكانَ فَضْلُ اللهِ عَلَيْكَ عَظِيماً** "and Allah’s grace on you has been very great" (4:113) is an expression of favor upon the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله.

His saying, the Exalted: **لا خَيْرَ فِي كَثِيرٍ مِنْ نَجْواهُمْ إِلَّا مَنْ أَمَرَ بِصَدَقَةٍ أَوْ مَعْرُوفٍ أَوْ إِصْلاحٍ بَيْنَ النَّاسِ** "There is no good in much of their secret talks, excepting him who enjoins charity or what is right or reconciliation between people" (4:114). Al-Raghib said: "**I held a secret talk with him** (ناجيته), meaning I spoke to him privately; its origin is to be alone with him in an elevated part of the land (نجوة)." (End of quote). Thus, **secret talk** (النجوى) is private conversation. It is sometimes applied to the participants in the secret talk themselves, as He, the Exalted, said: **وَإِذْ هُمْ نَجْوى** "and when they confer in secret" (17:47), meaning they are conversing privately.

In the speech—namely His saying: **لا خَيْرَ فِي كَثِيرٍ مِنْ نَجْواهُمْ**—there is a return to what preceded in His saying, the Exalted: **إِذْ يُبَيِّتُونَ ما لا يَرْضى مِنَ الْقَوْلِ** (etc.), based on the continuity of the verses. The explanation has been generalized to include all private conversation, whether it be by way of nocturnal plotting or otherwise, because the mentioned ruling—the absence of good in it—applies to all private conversation even if it is not by way of nocturnal plotting. Similar to it is His saying: **وَمَنْ يُشاقِقِ** (and whoever defies), without saying: "and whoever holds a secret talk for defiance," because the mentioned ruling for absolute defiance is more general than whether it is a secret talk or not.

The apparent sense of the exception is that it is disconnected (منقطع). The meaning is: But whoever enjoins such and such in it, then in what he enjoined there is something of good. He, the Exalted, called the call of the secret talk toward good an "enjoining" (أمراً), which is a type of metaphor. He, the Exalted, counted this good which the secret talk enjoins as three: charity (الصدقة), what is right (المعروف), and reconciliation between people (الإصلاح بين الناس). Perhaps the separation of charity from "what is right," despite it being one of its instances, is because it is the perfect instance in its natural need for secret talk, and it is mostly so.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَمَنْ يَفْعَلْ ذلِكَ ابْتِغاءَ مَرْضاتِ اللهِ** "and whoever does that seeking Allah’s pleasure" (4:114). This is a detailed explanation of the state of secret talk by another explanation in terms of the consequence of reward and punishment, so that the aspect of good in what is good of secret talk, and the absence of good in what is not good of it, may become clear.

Its summary is that the one who engages in secret talk is of two types: (one) who does that seeking Allah's pleasure, and this inevitably applies to one who calls to what is right or reconciliation between people out of seeking nearness to Allah; Allah, the Glorified, will soon reward him with a great reward. (Two) who does that to defy the Messenger and take a path other than the path of the believers; his recompense is divine respite and gradual leading to ruin (الاستدراج), then being cast into Hell, and an evil destination it is.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَمَنْ يُشاقِقِ الرَّسُولَ مِنْ بَعْدِ ما تَبَيَّنَ لَهُ الْهُدى وَيَتَّبِعْ غَيْرَ سَبِيلِ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ** "But whoever defies the Messenger after guidance has become distinct to him and follows a path other than that of the faithful" (4:115). **Defiance** (المشاقة) is from *shaqq*, which is a piece separated from a thing. Thus, defiance and discord (الشقاق) is your being in a side (شق) other than the side of your companion; it is a metaphor for opposition. What is meant by defying the Messenger after guidance has become distinct is opposing him and not obeying him. Accordingly, His saying: **وَيَتَّبِعْ غَيْرَ سَبِيلِ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ** is another explanation of defying the Messenger. What is meant by the "path of the faithful" (سبيل المؤمنين) is obeying the Messenger, for obeying him is obeying Allah. He, the Exalted, said: **مَنْ يُطِعِ الرَّسُولَ فَقَدْ أَطاعَ اللهَ** "Whoever obeys the Messenger has certainly obeyed Allah" (4:80).

Thus, the path of the faithful, insofar as they are a community united by faith, is the gathering upon the obedience of Allah and His Messenger—or if you wish, say: upon the obedience of His Messenger—for that is what preserves the unity of their path, as He, the Exalted, said: **وَكَيْفَ تَكْفُرُونَ وَأَنْتُمْ تُتْلى عَلَيْكُمْ آياتُ اللهِ وَفِيكُمْ رَسُولُهُ وَمَنْ يَعْتَصِمْ بِاللهِ فَقَدْ هُدِيَ إِلى صِراطٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اتَّقُوا اللهَ حَقَّ تُقاتِهِ وَلا تَمُوتُنَّ إِلَّا وَأَنْتُمْ مُسْلِمُونَ وَاعْتَصِمُوا بِحَبْلِ اللهِ جَمِيعاً وَلا تَفَرَّقُوا** "How can you disbelieve while Allah’s signs are recited to you and His Messenger is in your midst? Whoever holds fast to Allah has certainly been guided to a straight path. O you who have faith! Be wary of Allah with the wariness due to Him and do not die except as Muslims. Hold fast, all together, to Allah’s cord, and do not be divided" (3:101-103). The discussion on this verse has already passed in the third volume of this book. And He, the Exalted, said: **وَأَنَّ هذا صِراطِي مُسْتَقِيماً فَاتَّبِعُوهُ وَلا تَتَّبِعُوا السُّبُلَ فَتَفَرَّقَ بِكُمْ عَنْ سَبِيلِهِ ذلِكُمْ وَصَّاكُمْ بِهِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَتَّقُونَ** "This is indeed My straight path, so follow it, and do not follow [other] ways, for they will separate you from His way. This is what He has enjoined upon you so that you may be God-conscious." (6:153). If His way is the way of godwariness (التقوى), and the believers are those called to it, then their path collectively is the path of cooperation in godwariness, as He, the Exalted, said: **وَتَعاوَنُوا عَلَى الْبِرِّ وَالتَّقْوى وَلا تَعاوَنُوا عَلَى الْإِثْمِ وَالْعُدْوانِ** "Cooperate in piety and God-consciousness, and do not cooperate in sin and aggression" (5:2). The verse—as you see—prohibits disobeying Allah and breaking the staff of Islamic unity, which is the meaning we mentioned for the "path of the faithful."

So the meaning of the verse—namely His saying: **وَمَنْ يُشاقِقِ الرَّسُولَ مِنْ بَعْدِ ما تَبَيَّنَ لَهُ الْهُدى وَيَتَّبِعْ غَيْرَ سَبِيلِ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ**—returns to the meaning of His saying: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِذا تَناجَيْتُمْ فَلا تَتَناجَوْا بِالْإِثْمِ وَالْعُدْوانِ وَمَعْصِيَةِ الرَّسُولِ وَتَناجَوْا بِالْبِرِّ وَالتَّقْوى** "O you who have faith! When you confer privately, do not confer for sin and aggression and disobedience to the Messenger, but confer for piety and God-consciousness" (58:9).

And His saying: **نُوَلِّهِ ما تَوَلَّى** "We shall abandon him to what he has turned to," meaning We shall let him proceed upon what he has proceeded upon, and We shall assist him in what he has characterized himself with of following other than the path of the faithful, as He, the Exalted, said: **كُلًّا نُمِدُّ هؤُلاءِ وَهَؤُلاءِ مِنْ عَطاءِ رَبِّكَ وَما كانَ عَطاءُ رَبِّكَ مَحْظُوراً** "To these and to those—to all We extend the bounty of your Lord, and the bounty of your Lord is not confined." (17:20).

And His saying: **وَنُصْلِهِ جَهَنَّمَ وَساءَتْ مَصِيراً** "and We shall make him enter Hell, and it is an evil destination" (4:115). Its conjunction with the "and" (الواو) indicates that the whole—namely, abandoning him to what he has turned to and making him enter Hell—is a single divine matter, some of whose parts are worldly, which is abandoning him to what he has turned to, and some are of the hereafter, which is making him enter Hell, and an evil destination it is.

His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَغْفِرُ أَنْ يُشْرَكَ بِهِ** "Indeed Allah does not forgive that any partner be ascribed to Him" (to the end of the verse) (4:116). The apparent sense of the verse is that it is in the position of providing the reason for His saying in the previous verse: **نُوَلِّهِ ما تَوَلَّى وَنُصْلِهِ جَهَنَّمَ**, based on the continuity of the verses. Thus, the verse indicates that defying the Messenger is polytheism (شرك) against Allah the Great, and that Allah does not forgive that any partner be ascribed to Him. This might also be derived from His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا وَصَدُّوا عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللهِ وَشَاقُّوا الرَّسُولَ مِنْ بَعْدِ ما تَبَيَّنَ لَهُمُ الْهُدى لَنْ يَضُرُّوا اللهَ شَيْئاً وَسَيُحْبِطُ أَعْمالَهُمْ يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا أَطِيعُوا اللهَ وَأَطِيعُوا الرَّسُولَ وَلا تُبْطِلُوا أَعْمالَكُمْ إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا وَصَدُّوا عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللهِ ثُمَّ ماتُوا وَهُمْ كُفَّارٌ فَلَنْ يَغْفِرَ اللهُ لَهُمْ** "Indeed those who disbelieve and bar from the way of Allah and defy the Messenger after guidance has become distinct to them, will not harm Allah in the least, and He will make their works fail. O you who have faith! Obey Allah and obey the Messenger, and do not make your works void. Indeed those who disbelieve and bar from the way of Allah and then die as faithless, Allah will never forgive them." (47:32-34). For the apparent sense of the third verse is that it is a justification for what is in the second verse of the command to obey Allah and obey His Messenger; thus, exiting from the obedience of Allah and the obedience of His Messenger is a disbelief (كفر) that is never forgiven, and that is polytheism (الشرك).

The context suggests that the attachment of His saying: **وَيَغْفِرُ ما دُونَ ذلِكَ لِمَنْ يَشاءُ** "but He forgives what is besides that to whomever He wishes" to His saying: **إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَغْفِرُ أَنْ يُشْرَكَ بِهِ** is only to complete the explanation and to convey the magnitude of this ill-fated disobedience—namely, defying the Messenger. Some discussion on the verse has already passed at the end of the fourth volume of this book.

His saying, the Exalted: **إِنْ يَدْعُونَ مِنْ دُونِهِ إِلَّا إِناثاً** "They invoke none but females besides Him" (4:117). **Females** (إناثاً) is the plural of *unthā* (female). It is said: "The iron became female" (أنث الحديد), meaning it became passive and soft. "The place became female" (أنث المكان) when it was quick to sprout and was good. Thus, it contains the meaning of passivity (الانفعال) and being affected. By this, the female among animals was named *unthā*. The idols and every object of worship besides Allah were named "females" because they are passive recipients (قابلات منفعلات) that do not have the power to do anything of what their worshippers expect from them—as has been said. He, the Exalted, said: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ تَدْعُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ لَنْ يَخْلُقُوا ذُباباً وَلَوِ اجْتَمَعُوا لَهُ وَإِنْ يَسْلُبْهُمُ الذُّبابُ شَيْئاً لا يَسْتَنْقِذُوهُ مِنْهُ ضَعُفَ الطَّالِبُ وَالْمَطْلُوبُ ما قَدَرُوا اللهَ حَقَّ قَدْرِهِ إِنَّ اللهَ لَقَوِيٌّ عَزِيزٌ** "Indeed those whom you invoke besides Allah will never create [even] a fly even if they were to combine their efforts to do so! And should a fly take away something from them, they cannot recover it from it. Feeble are the seeker and the sought! They do not regard Allah with the regard due to Him. Indeed Allah is all-strong, all-mighty." (22:73-74). And He said: **وَاتَّخَذُوا مِنْ دُونِهِ آلِهَةً لا يَخْلُقُونَ شَيْئاً وَهُمْ يُخْلَقُونَ وَلا يَمْلِكُونَ لِأَنْفُسِهِمْ ضَرًّا وَلا نَفْعاً وَلا يَمْلِكُونَ مَوْتاً وَلا حَياةً وَلا نُشُوراً** "They have taken gods besides Him who create nothing and are themselves created, and who have no control over their own harm or benefit, and have no control over death, life, or resurrection." (25:3).

So it appears that what is meant by "femininity" (الأنوثة) is pure passivity (الانفعال المحض), which is the status of the creature when compared to the Creator, exalted is His Name. This view is preferable to what has been said: that what is meant are al-Lat, al-‘Uzza, and Manat the third, and their like, as every tribe had an idol they called the "female of the children of so-and-so," either due to the femininity of their names or because they were inanimate objects, and inanimate objects are treated as feminine in language.

The reason for this preference is that the latter view does not very well suit the restriction occurring in His saying: **إِنْ يَدْعُونَ مِنْ دُونِهِ إِلَّا إِناثاً**, for among those invoked besides Allah are those who are male, not female, such as Jesus the Messiah, Brahma, and Buddha.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَإِنْ يَدْعُونَ إِلَّا شَيْطاناً مَرِيداً** "and they invoke none but a froward Satan" (4:117). The **froward** (المريد) is the one devoid of all good, or absolutely devoid. Al-Baydawi said: "The *mārid* and the *marīd* is the one to whom no good attaches. The origin of the root is for smoothness; from it is 'a smoothed palace' (صرح ممرد), 'a beardless youth' (غلام أمرد), and 'a bare tree' (شجرة مرداء) for one whose leaves have fallen off." (End of quote).

It appears that the sentence is an explanation for the preceding sentence, for "invoking" (الدعوة) is a metaphor for worship (العبادة), because worship only arose among people due to the call (الدعوة) of need. Allah the Exalted has named obedience "worship," saying: **أَلَمْ أَعْهَدْ إِلَيْكُمْ يا بَنِي آدَمَ أَنْ لا تَعْبُدُوا الشَّيْطانَ إِنَّهُ لَكُمْ عَدُوٌّ مُبِينٌ وَأَنِ اعْبُدُونِي** "Did I not enjoin you, O Children of Adam, that you should not worship Satan? He is indeed your manifest enemy. And that you should worship Me?" (36:60-61). Thus, the meaning of the sentence boils down to the fact that their worship of every object of worship besides Allah is a worship and an invocation by them of the froward Satan, because it is an obedience to him.

His saying, the Exalted: **لَعَنَهُ اللهُ** "whom Allah has cursed" (4:118). **Cursing** (اللعن) is the removal from mercy; it is a second description of Satan and is in the position of a justification for the first description.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَقالَ لَأَتَّخِذَنَّ مِنْ عِبادِكَ نَصِيباً مَفْرُوضاً** "and who said, ‘I will surely take of Your servants a settled share’" (4:118). It is as if it is an allusion to what Allah the Exalted recounted of him saying: **فَبِعِزَّتِكَ لَأُغْوِيَنَّهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ إِلَّا عِبادَكَ مِنْهُمُ الْمُخْلَصِينَ** "By Your might, I will surely pervert them all, except Your devoted servants among them." (38:82-83). In His saying: **مِنْ عِبادِكَ** (of Your servants) is an affirmation that they are, despite that, His servants; they do not strip themselves of this status, and He is their Lord who judges among them as He wills.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَأُضِلَّنَّهُمْ وَلَأُمَنِّيَنَّهُمْ** (to the end of the verse) (4:119). **Slitting** (التبتيل) is cutting. It applies to what has been reported: that the Arabs of the Age of Ignorance used to slit the ears of the *baḥā’ir* and *sawā’ib* (types of camels) to prohibit their meat.

All these enumerated matters are misguidance (ضلال). Thus, mentioning misguidance along with them is of the nature of mentioning the general and then mentioning some of its instances due to a specific concern for them. He says: I will surely lead them astray by occupying them with the worship of other than Allah and the commission of disobediences; and I will surely delude them by occupying them with hopes and desires (الأماني) that distract them from occupying themselves with the obligation of their status and what concerns them of their affair; and I will surely command them to slit the ears of cattle and prohibit what Allah, the Glorified, has made lawful; and I will surely command them to change the creation of Allah (تغيير خلق الله), which applies to things like castration and types of mutilation (المثلة), sodomy, and lesbianism.

It is not far-fetched that what is meant by "changing the creation of Allah" is exiting from the ruling of the primordial nature (الفطرة) and abandoning the upright religion (الدين الحنيف). He, the Exalted, said: **فَأَقِمْ وَجْهَكَ لِلدِّينِ حَنِيفاً فِطْرَتَ اللهِ الَّتِي فَطَرَ النَّاسَ عَلَيْها لا تَبْدِيلَ لِخَلْقِ اللهِ ذلِكَ الدِّينُ الْقَيِّمُ** "So set your face on the religion as a Hanif, with the nature of Allah which He has created in mankind. There is no altering Allah’s creation. That is the upright religion." (30:30).

Then He, the Exalted, counted the call of Satan—which is obedience to him in what he commands—as taking him as a guardian (ولياً), saying: **وَمَنْ يَتَّخِذِ الشَّيْطانَ وَلِيًّا مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ فَقَدْ خَسِرَ خُسْراناً مُبِيناً** "Whoever takes Satan as a guardian instead of Allah has certainly incurred a manifest loss." (4:119). He did not say: "and whoever has Satan as a guardian," to signal what the previous verses signal: that the Guardian (الولي) is Allah, and there is no guardianship (ولاية) for any other over anything, even if he is taken as a guardian.

His saying, the Exalted: **يَعِدُهُمْ وَيُمَنِّيهِمْ وَما يَعِدُهُمُ الشَّيْطانُ إِلَّا غُرُوراً** "He makes them promises and gives them [false] hopes, and Satan does not promise them anything but delusion." (4:120). The apparent context is that it is a justification for His saying in the previous verse: **فَقَدْ خَسِرَ خُسْراناً مُبِيناً**. And what loss is more manifest than the loss of one who exchanges true happiness and the perfection of creation for false promises and illusory desires? He, the Exalted, said: **وَالَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا أَعْمالُهُمْ كَسَرابٍ بِقِيعَةٍ يَحْسَبُهُ الظَّمْآنُ ماءً حَتَّى إِذا جاءَهُ لَمْ يَجِدْهُ شَيْئاً وَوَجَدَ اللهَ عِنْدَهُ فَوَفَّاهُ حِسابَهُ وَاللهُ سَرِيعُ الْحِسابِ** "As for the faithless, their works are like a mirage in a plain, which the thirsty man supposes to be water. When he comes to it, he finds it to be nothing; but there he finds Allah, who pays him his full account, and Allah is swift at reckoning." (24:39).

As for the promises (المواعيد), they are the Satanic whisperings (الوساوس) without intermediary. As for the desires (الأماني), they are what branch out from his whisperings of what the imagination (الوهم) finds pleasurable of the imaginaries. For this reason, He said: **وَما يَعِدُهُمُ الشَّيْطانُ إِلَّا غُرُوراً**, thus calling the promise a "delusion" (غروراً) but not the desire, as is not hidden.

Then He explained the consequence of their state by His saying: **أُولئِكَ مَأْواهُمْ جَهَنَّمُ وَلا يَجِدُونَ عَنْها مَحِيصاً** "The abode of such is Hell, and they will not find any escape from it." (4:121). That is, no turning away or place of flight, from *ḥāṣa* when one turns away.

Then He mentioned what contrasts with their state, which is the state of the believers, to complete the explanation, saying: **وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ سَنُدْخِلُهُمْ جَنَّاتٍ** "But those who have faith and do righteous deeds, We shall admit them into gardens" (to the end of the verse) (4:122). In the verses, there is a shift (التفات) from the context of speaking in the first person plural to the third person. The general reason for it is to signal the majesty and greatness of the status by placing the Name of Majesty (لفظ الجلالة) in the place of the pronoun of the first person plural in what requires this signal, until the purpose is fulfilled, then returning to the previous context which was the original. This occurs in His saying: **سَنُدْخِلُهُمْ جَنَّاتٍ**. In that is another subtle point: the signal of the proximity of presence and His not being veiled, the Exalted, from His believing servants, and He is their Guardian.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَعْدَ اللهِ حَقًّا وَمَنْ أَصْدَقُ مِنَ اللهِ قِيلاً** "a true promise of Allah; and who is truer in speech than Allah?" (4:122). In this is a contrast to what was mentioned regarding the promise of Satan—that it is nothing but delusion. Thus, the promise of Allah was truth (حقاً), and His speech was sincerity (صدقاً).

His saying, the Exalted: **لَيْسَ بِأَمانِيِّكُمْ وَلا أَمانِيِّ أَهْلِ الْكِتابِ** — "It is not by your fancies, nor by the fancies of the People of the Book" (4:123) — is a return to the beginning of the discourse and serves as a derived conclusion summarizing the detailed discussion. This is because it is gathered from what has been narrated regarding the actions and words of some believers, and their persistence with the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to favor them, support them, and assist them against others in the disputes and quarrels occurring between them, that they believe their faith grants them a special dignity (الكرامة) before Allah, the Glorified, and a right over the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). They believe this right necessitates that Allah and His Messenger favor them and grant their side victory over others, whether they are in the right or the wrong, and whether the judgment is just or unjust. This is similar to the view held by the followers of leaders of misguidance (أئمة الضلال), and the entourages, confidants, and lackeys of oppressive rulers; such a person considers himself to be doing a favor for his leader or chief even while submitting to and obeying him, believing he possesses a dignity that obliges the leader to favor him and prefer him over others arbitrarily (تحكما).

The People of the Book (أهل الكتاب) held a similar view, as Allah, the Exalted, has narrated about them in His Book. He, the Exalted, says: **وَقالَتِ الْيَهُودُ وَالنَّصارى نَحْنُ أَبْناءُ اللهِ وَأَحِبَّاؤُهُ** — "The Jews and the Christians say, ‘We are Allah’s children and His beloved ones’" (5:18); and He, the Exalted, says: **وَقالُوا كُونُوا هُوداً أَوْ نَصارى تَهْتَدُوا** — "And they say, ‘Be Jews or Christians and you will be guided’" (2:135); and He, the Exalted, says: **قالُوا لَيْسَ عَلَيْنا فِي الْأُمِّيِّينَ سَبِيلٌ** — "They say, ‘We have no obligation toward the gentiles’" (3:75).

Thus, Allah refuted this group of believers in their claim and coupled them with the People of the Book, labeling these claims as "fancies" (الأماني) metaphorically (استعارة). This is because they are like fancies—nothing more than pleasurable mental images that have no reality in the external world (الأعيان). He said: It is not according to your fancies, O assembly of Muslims—or a group among the Muslims—nor the fancies of the People of the Book. Rather, the matter revolves around action (العمل): if it is good, then the result is good; and if it is evil, then the result is evil. He mentioned the evil deed before the good deed because the core of their error lay therein.

His saying, the Exalted: **مَنْ يَعْمَلْ سُوءاً يُجْزَ بِهِ وَلا يَجِدْ لَهُ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ وَلِيًّا وَلا نَصِيراً** — "Whoever commits evil shall be requited for it, and he will not find for himself any guardian or helper besides Allah" (4:123). This sentence is brought without a connective (الفصل) because it stands as an answer to an implied question. The implication is: If entering the sanctuary of Islam and faith (الإيمان) does not automatically bring every good to a person and preserve his interests in life—and likewise for Judaism and Christianity—then what is the path? And what will be the end of man's state? Thus, it was said: "Whoever commits evil shall be requited for it, and he will not find for himself any guardian or helper besides Allah, and whoever performs righteous deeds..." (etc.).

His saying, **مَنْ يَعْمَلْ سُوءاً يُجْزَ بِهِ** — "Whoever commits evil shall be requited for it" — is absolute (مطلق). It includes the worldly requital (الجزاء الدنيوي) established by the Islamic Law (الشريعة), such as retaliation (القصاص) for the criminal, amputation for the thief, and flogging or stoning for the adulterer, among other administrative rulings (أحكام السياسات). It also includes the hereafter's requital (الجزاء الأخروي) which Allah, the Exalted, has promised in His Book and through the tongue of His Prophet.

This generalization (التعميم) is what befits the context of these noble verses and applies to them. It has been mentioned regarding the occasion of revelation (شأن النزول) that the verses were revealed concerning a theft committed by someone who then accused a Jew or a Muslim, after which they pressured the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to rule against the accused.

His saying, **وَلا يَجِدْ لَهُ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ وَلِيًّا وَلا نَصِيراً** — "and he will not find for himself any guardian or helper besides Allah" — includes any guardian (الولي) or helper (النصير) who might avert the evil requital from him in this world, such as the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) or the master of affairs (ولي الأمر), or through proximity to them, or the dignity of Islam and religion. The requital legislated by Allah cannot be averted from the evildoer by any averter. It also includes the guardian and helper who might avert the evil requital in the hereafter, except for what is covered by the following verse.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَمَنْ يَعْمَلْ مِنَ الصَّالِحاتِ مِنْ ذَكَرٍ أَوْ أُنْثى وَهُوَ مُؤْمِنٌ فَأُولئِكَ يَدْخُلُونَ الْجَنَّةَ وَلا يُظْلَمُونَ نَقِيراً** — "And whoever performs righteous deeds, whether male or female, should he be faithful, such shall enter paradise and they will not be wronged [so much as] the speck on a date-stone" (4:124). This is the second part, containing the requital for the performer of righteous deeds (العمل الصالح), which is Paradise. However, Allah, the Glorified, placed a condition upon it that necessitates a restriction in the actualization of the requital, while generalizing it from another aspect that necessitates expansiveness.

He conditioned the requital of Paradise upon the performer of the righteous deed being a believer (مؤمن), for the good requital is only in exchange for a righteous deed, and the disbeliever has no [valid] deed. He, the Exalted, says: **وَلَوْ أَشْرَكُوا لَحَبِطَ عَنْهُمْ ما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ** — "And if they had ascribed partners [to Him], what they used to do would have come to nothing" (6:88); and He, the Exalted, says: **أُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا بِآياتِ رَبِّهِمْ وَلِقائِهِ فَحَبِطَتْ أَعْمالُهُمْ فَلا نُقِيمُ لَهُمْ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ وَزْناً** — "They are those who deny the signs of their Lord and the encounter with Him. So their works have failed, and on the Day of Resurrection We will not set up any balance for them" (18:105).

He, the Exalted, said: **وَمَنْ يَعْمَلْ مِنَ الصَّالِحاتِ** — "And whoever performs [some] of the righteous deeds" — using the partitive "of" (من التبعيضية), which is an expansion (توسعة) in the promise of Paradise. Had it been said, "And whoever performs the righteous deeds"—and the context is one of precision in requital—it would have implied that Paradise is only for one who believes and performs *every* righteous deed. However, Divine Grace generalized the good requital for whoever believes and performs *some* righteous deeds; He then rectifies what remains of the righteous deeds or the sins committed through repentance (التوبة) or intercession (الشفاعة), as He, the Exalted, says: **إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَغْفِرُ أَنْ يُشْرَكَ بِهِ وَيَغْفِرُ ما دُونَ ذلِكَ لِمَنْ يَشاءُ** — "Indeed Allah does not forgive that any partner should be ascribed to Him, but He forgives anything besides that to whomever He wishes" (4:116). The detailed discussion on repentance and His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّمَا التَّوْبَةُ عَلَى اللهِ** — "Acceptance of repentance by Allah is only..." (4:17) was presented in the fourth volume, and on intercession (الشفاعة) — *the act of mediating on behalf of others before Allah* — under His saying, the Exalted: **وَاتَّقُوا يَوْماً لا تَجْزِي نَفْسٌ عَنْ نَفْسٍ شَيْئاً** — "And beware of a day when no soul will compensate for another soul in any way" (2:48) in the first volume of this book.

He, the Exalted, said: **مِنْ ذَكَرٍ أَوْ أُنْثى** — "whether male or female" — thus generalizing the ruling for both male and female without any distinction whatsoever. This is contrary to what was claimed by the ancients among the religious communities and sects (أهل الملل والنحل), such as the Indians, Egyptians, and other polytheists, who believed that women have no [valid] deeds and no reward for their virtues. It also counters what appeared in Judaism and Christianity—that dignity and honor belong to men, while women are lowly before Allah, deficient in creation, and losers in terms of wages and reward. The Arabs were no different regarding these beliefs. Thus, Allah, the Exalted, equated the two groups with His saying: **مِنْ ذَكَرٍ أَوْ أُنْثى**.

Perhaps this is the secret behind following His saying, **فأُولئِكَ يَدْخُلُونَ الْجَنَّةَ** — "such shall enter paradise" — with His saying, **وَلا يُظْلَمُونَ نَقِيراً** — "and they will not be wronged [so much as] the speck on a date-stone." This is so the first sentence indicates that women have a share in the reward just like men, and the second sentence indicates that there is no difference between them in it regarding increase or decrease, as He, the Exalted, says: **فَاسْتَجابَ لَهُمْ رَبُّهُمْ أَنِّي لا أُضِيعُ عَمَلَ عامِلٍ مِنْكُمْ مِنْ ذَكَرٍ أَوْ أُنْثى بَعْضُكُمْ مِنْ بَعْضٍ** — "Then their Lord answered them, ‘I do not waste the work of any worker among you, whether male or female; you are all on the same footing’" (3:195).

His saying, the Exalted: **وَمَنْ أَحْسَنُ دِيناً مِمَّنْ أَسْلَمَ وَجْهَهُ لِلَّهِ وَهُوَ مُحْسِنٌ** — "And who has a better religion than him who submits his will to Allah, being virtuous" (4:125) (to the end of the verse). It is as if this is a rebuttal to an implied objection. The objection is: If the submission (إسلام) of the Muslim or the faith (إيمان) of the People of the Book has no effect in bringing good to him and preserving his interests—and in short, if faith in Allah and His signs counts for nothing and its existence and non-existence are equal—then what is the dignity of Islam and what is the merit of faith?

The answer was that the dignity of religion (الدين) is a matter beyond doubt or suspicion, and its beauty is not hidden from any person of intellect. This is His saying: **وَمَنْ أَحْسَنُ دِيناً** — "And who has a better religion," where He affirms this through a rhetorical question (الاستفهام) as an established fact. For man has no escape from religion, and the best religion is the submission of the face to Allah, to Whom belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth, and to be humble before Him with the humility of servitude (العبودية), and to act according to the requirements of the creed of Abraham, the upright (حنيفاً)—which is the primordial creed (الملة الفطرية). Allah, the Glorified, took Abraham—who was the first to submit his face to Allah while being virtuous and followed the upright creed—as a friend (خليلاً).

However, one should not imagine that Divine friendship (الخلة الإلهية) is like the friendship prevalent among people, which rules between them in every truth and falsehood, opening the door for them to act haphazardly and arbitrarily. For Allah, the Glorified, is the Owner (مالك) who is not owned, and the Encompasser (محيط) who is not encompassed. This is unlike the masters, leaders, and kings among men, for they do not own anything of their slaves and subjects except that the subjects own something of them in return; they overpower some by means of others, and they rule over one group through the support of another. Therefore, they do not remain stable in their positions if their will opposes the will of all; rather, they fall from their positions and their weakness becomes apparent.

From here, the reason for following His saying, **وَمَنْ أَحْسَنُ دِيناً** (etc.) with His saying, **وَلِلَّهِ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَما فِي الْأَرْضِ وَكانَ اللهُ بِكُلِّ شَيْءٍ مُحِيطاً** — "To Allah belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth, and Allah encompasses all things" (4:126) becomes clear.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi: The reason for its revelation (meaning His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّا أَنْزَلْنا إِلَيْكَ الْكِتابَ** — the verses) is that a group from the Ansar from the tribe of Banu Ubayriq—three brothers who were hypocrites: Bashir, Bishr, and Mubashshir—tunneled into the house of the uncle of Qatadah ibn al-Nu'man (Qatadah was a veteran of Badr) and took out food he had prepared for his family, a sword, and a coat of mail.

Qatadah complained about this to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), saying: "O Messenger of Allah, a group tunneled into my uncle's house and took food he had prepared for his family, a sword, and a coat of mail, and they are a family of evil." There was a believing man with them in opinion called Labid ibn Sahl. The Banu Ubayriq said to Qatadah: "This is the work of Labid ibn Sahl." When this reached Labid, he took his sword and went out to them, saying: "O Banu Ubayriq, do you accuse me of theft? You are more deserving of it than I; you are the hypocrites who lampoon the Messenger of Allah and attribute it to the Quraysh. You shall clarify this or I shall fill my sword with you!" They humored him and said: "Go back, may Allah have mercy on you, for you are innocent of that."

The Banu Ubayriq then went to a man of their kinsmen called Usayd ibn Urwah, who was an eloquent orator. He went to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and said: "O Messenger of Allah, Qatadah ibn al-Nu'man has targeted a household among us—people of honor, status, and lineage—and accused them of theft, charging them with what is not in them." The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) became distressed by this. Qatadah came to him, and the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) turned to him and said: "You targeted a household of honor, status, and lineage and accused them of theft," and he rebuked him severely. Qatadah became distressed by this and returned to his uncle, saying: "I wish I had died and not spoken to the Messenger of Allah, for he spoke to me in a way I disliked." His uncle said: "Allah is the one whose help is sought (الله المستعان)."

Then Allah revealed to His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) regarding this: **إِنَّا أَنْزَلْنا إِلَيْكَ الْكِتابَ بِالْحَقِّ** — (until He said) — **إِذْ يُبَيِّتُونَ ما لا يَرْضى مِنَ الْقَوْلِ** — "when they conspire at night in words that do not please Him" (4:108). (Al-Qummi said: Meaning the action), so "words" (القول) stood in place of "action" (الفعل).

**ها أَنْتُمْ هؤُلاءِ جادَلْتُمْ عَنْهُمْ فِي الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا** — "Aha!
