There you are, pleading for them in the life of this world" (4:109) — (until He said) — **وَمَنْ يَكْسِبْ خَطِيئَةً أَوْ إِثْماً ثُمَّ يَرْمِ بِهِ بَرِيئاً** — "But whoever commits a sin or a crime and then blames it on an innocent person" (4:112). (Al-Qummi said: Labid ibn Sahl) — **فَقَدِ احْتَمَلَ بُهْتاناً وَإِثْماً مُبِيناً** — "has certainly saddled himself with calumny and a flagrant sin."

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi: In a narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him): A person from the closest kin of Bashir said: "Come with us to the Messenger of Allah," and they said: "We will speak to him regarding our companion—or we will offer an excuse that our companion is innocent." When Allah revealed: **يَسْتَخْفُونَ مِنَ اللهِ** — (to His saying) — **وَكِيلاً** — "They hide from people, but they cannot hide from Allah... and who will be their defender?" (4:108-109), the kinsmen of Bishr came and said: "O Bishr, seek forgiveness from Allah and repent to Him for the sins." He said: "By Him by whom I swear, no one stole it but Labid." Then was revealed: **وَمَنْ يَكْسِبْ خَطِيئَةً أَوْ إِثْماً ثُمَّ يَرْمِ بِهِ بَرِيئاً فَقَدِ احْتَمَلَ بُهْتاناً وَإِثْماً مُبِيناً**.

Then Bishr committed disbelief (كفر) and fled to Mecca. Regarding the group who offered excuses for Bishr and came to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to excuse him, Allah revealed: **وَلَوْ لا فَضْلُ اللهِ عَلَيْكَ وَرَحْمَتُهُ لَهَمَّتْ طائِفَةٌ مِنْهُمْ أَنْ يُضِلُّوكَ** — (to His saying) — **وَكانَ فَضْلُ اللهِ عَلَيْكَ عَظِيماً** — "Were it not for Allah’s grace and His mercy on you, a group of them were determined to lead you astray... and Allah’s grace on you has been very great" (4:113).

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*: Al-Tirmidhi, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, Abu al-Shaykh, and al-Hakim (who authenticated it) narrated from Qatadah ibn al-Nu'man who said: There was a household among us called "Banu Ubayriq": Bishr, Bashir, and Mubashshir. Bishr was a hypocritical man who composed poetry lampooning the companions of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), then he would attribute it to some of the Arabs, saying: "So-and-so said such-and-such." When the companions of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) heard that poetry, they would say: "By Allah, no one says this poetry but this wicked man." He said:

*Is it that every time men compose a poem,*
*They whisper and say: the son of al-Ubayriq said it?*

He said: They were a household of need and poverty in the Pre-Islamic period (الجاهلية) and in Islam. The food of the people in Medina was only dates and barley. If a man was wealthy and a caravan (ضافطة) arrived from Syria with white flour (الدرمك), he would buy from it and keep it for himself, while the food for the family remained dates and barley.

A caravan arrived from Syria, and my uncle Rifa'ah ibn Zayd bought a load of white flour and placed it in an upper room (مشربة) of his. In the room was his weaponry: two coats of mail, their swords, and what pertained to them. Someone attacked during the night, tunneled into the room, and took the food and the weaponry. When morning came, my uncle Rifa'ah came to me and said: "O nephew, know that we have been attacked this night; our room was tunneled into and our food and weaponry were taken."

He said: We searched the neighborhood and asked, and it was said to us: "We saw the Banu Ubayriq lighting a fire this night, and we believe it was over some of your food." He said: The Banu Ubayriq had said—while we were asking in the neighborhood: "By Allah, we do not think your culprit is anyone but Labid ibn Sahl"—a man among us of righteousness and Islam. When Labid heard that, he unsheathed his sword, then came to the Banu Ubayriq and said: "I steal? By Allah, this sword shall mix with you, or you shall clarify this theft!" They said: "Away from us, O man, by Allah you are not the one." We continued asking in the neighborhood until we had no doubt they were the culprits. My uncle said to me: "O nephew, if only you would go to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and mention that to him!"

Qatadah said: I went to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and said: "O Messenger of Allah, a household among us of harshness targeted my uncle Rifa'ah ibn Zayd, tunneled into his room, and took his weaponry and food. Let them return our weaponry to us; as for the food, we have no need for it." The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "I shall look into that."

When the Banu Ubayriq heard that, they went to a man of theirs called "Asir ibn Urwah" and spoke to him about it. A group of the people of the neighborhood gathered with him and they went to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and said: "O Messenger of Allah, Qatadah ibn al-Nu'man and his uncle have targeted a household among us—people of Islam and righteousness—accusing them of theft without evidence or proof."

Qatadah said: I went to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and spoke to him, and he said: "You targeted a household of whom Islam and righteousness are mentioned, accusing them of theft without evidence or proof?"

Qatadah said: I returned and wished that I had given up some of my wealth and not spoken to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) about it. My uncle Rifa'ah came to me and said: "O nephew, what have you done?" I informed him of what the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said to me. He said: "Allah is the one whose help is sought."

We did not wait long before the Quran was revealed: **إِنَّا أَنْزَلْنا إِلَيْكَ الْكِتابَ بِالْحَقِّ لِتَحْكُمَ بَيْنَ النَّاسِ بِما أَراكَ اللهُ وَلا تَكُنْ لِلْخائِنِينَ خَصِيماً** — "Indeed We have sent down the Book to you with the truth, so that you may judge between the people by what Allah has shown you. Do not be an advocate for the treacherous" (4:105)—referring to the Banu Ubayriq—**وَاسْتَغْفِرِ اللهَ** — "and plead to Allah for forgiveness"—meaning for what you said to Qatadah—**إِنَّ اللهَ كانَ غَفُوراً رَحِيماً وَلا تُجادِلْ عَنِ الَّذِينَ يَخْتانُونَ أَنْفُسَهُمْ** — "Indeed Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful. And do not plead for those who deceive themselves" (4:106-107)—(to His saying)—**ثُمَّ يَسْتَغْفِرِ اللهَ يَجِدِ اللهَ غَفُوراً رَحِيماً** — "then pleads to Allah for forgiveness, he will find Allah all-forgiving, all-merciful" (4:110)—meaning if they had sought forgiveness from Allah, He would have forgiven them—**وَمَنْ يَكْسِبْ خَطِيئَةً أَوْ إِثْماً** — (to His saying) — **فَقَدِ احْتَمَلَ بُهْتاناً وَإِثْماً مُبِيناً** —referring to their accusation against Labid—**وَلَوْ لا فَضْلُ اللهِ عَلَيْكَ وَرَحْمَتُهُ لَهَمَّتْ طائِفَةٌ مِنْهُمْ أَنْ يُضِلُّوكَ** —meaning Asir ibn Urwah and his companions—(to His saying)—**فَسَوْفَ نُؤْتِيهِ أَجْراً عَظِيماً** — "We shall give him a great reward" (4:114). When the Quran was revealed, the weaponry was brought to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and he returned it to Rifa'ah.

Qatadah said: When I brought the weaponry to my uncle—and he was an old man who had aged in the Pre-Islamic period, and I used to think his Islam was deficient—when I brought him the weaponry, he said: "O nephew, it is for the sake of Allah." Then I knew that his Islam was sincere.

When the Quran was revealed, Bishr joined the polytheists and stayed with Sulafah bint Sa'd. Then Allah revealed: **وَمَنْ يُشاقِقِ الرَّسُولَ مِنْ بَعْدِ ما تَبَيَّنَ لَهُ الْهُدى وَيَتَّبِعْ غَيْرَ سَبِيلِ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ نُوَلِّهِ ما تَوَلَّى** — (to His saying) — **ضَلالاً بَعِيداً** — "But whoever defies the Apostle after guidance has become distinct to him and follows a path other than that of the faithful, We shall abandon him to what he has turned to... a remote error" (4:115-116). When he stayed with Sulafah, Hassan ibn Thabit lampooned her with some verses of poetry. She took his saddle, placed it on her head, then went out and threw it in the valley (الأبطح), saying: "You brought me Hassan's poetry? You never brought me any good."

I say: This meaning is narrated through other chains as well.

In it: Ibn Jarir narrated from Ibn Zayd regarding the verse: A man stole a coat of mail made of iron during the time of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and threw it upon a Jew. The Jew said: "By Allah, I did not steal it, O Abu al-Qasim, but it was thrown upon me." The man who stole had neighbors who were acquitting him and blaming the Jew, saying: "O Messenger of Allah, this Jew is wicked; he disbelieves in Allah and in what you have brought," until the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) leaned toward them with some words.

Then Allah rebuked him for that, saying: **إِنَّا أَنْزَلْنا إِلَيْكَ الْكِتابَ بِالْحَقِّ لِتَحْكُمَ بَيْنَ النَّاسِ بِما أَراكَ اللهُ وَلا تَكُنْ لِلْخائِنِينَ خَصِيماً وَاسْتَغْفِرِ اللهَ** —for what you said to this Jew—**إِنَّ اللهَ كانَ غَفُوراً رَحِيماً**. Then He turned to his neighbors and said: **ها أَنْتُمْ هؤُلاءِ جادَلْتُمْ عَنْهُمْ** — (to His saying) — **وَكِيلاً**. Then He offered repentance, saying: **وَمَنْ يَعْمَلْ سُوءاً أَوْ يَظْلِمْ نَفْسَهُ ثُمَّ يَسْتَغْفِرِ اللهَ يَجِدِ اللهَ غَفُوراً رَحِيماً وَوَمَنْ يَكْسِبْ إِثْماً فَإِنَّما يَكْسِبُهُ عَلى نَفْسِهِ** — "And whoever commits a sin, commits it only against himself" (4:111)—so why do you, O people, involve yourselves in the sin of this man, speaking on his behalf? **وَمَنْ يَكْسِبْ خَطِيئَةً أَوْ إِثْماً ثُمَّ يَرْمِ بِهِ بَرِيئاً** —even if he is a polytheist—**فَقَدِ احْتَمَلَ بُهْتاناً** — (to His saying) — **وَمَنْ يُشاقِقِ الرَّسُولَ مِنْ بَعْدِ ما تَبَيَّنَ لَهُ الْهُدى**. He said: He refused to accept the repentance that Allah offered him and went out to the polytheists in Mecca. He tunneled into a house to steal from it, but Allah caused it to collapse on him and it killed him.

I say: This meaning is also narrated through many chains with slight variations.

In the *Tafsir* of al-Ayyashi, from the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله): "There is no servant who commits a sin, then stands and performs ablution and seeks forgiveness from Allah for his sin, except that it becomes a right upon Allah to forgive him, because He says: **مَنْ يَعْمَلْ سُوءاً أَوْ يَظْلِمْ نَفْسَهُ ثُمَّ يَسْتَغْفِرِ اللهَ يَجِدِ اللهَ غَفُوراً رَحِيماً**."

And he said: "Indeed, Allah tries the servant while He loves him so that He may hear his supplication." And he said: "Allah would not open the door of supplication and close the door of response, for He says: **ادْعُونِي أَسْتَجِبْ لَكُمْ** — 'Call Me, I will answer you' (40:60). And He would not open the door of repentance and close the door of forgiveness while He says: **مَنْ يَعْمَلْ سُوءاً أَوْ يَظْلِمْ نَفْسَهُ ثُمَّ يَسْتَغْفِرِ اللهَ يَجِدِ اللهَ غَفُوراً رَحِيماً**."

In it, from Abdullah ibn Hammad al-Ansari from Abdullah ibn Sinan, who said: Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) said: "Backbiting (الغيبة) is to say about your brother what is in him of that which Allah has concealed for him. But if you say what is not in him, then that is the saying of Allah: **فَقَدِ احْتَمَلَ بُهْتاناً وَإِثْماً مُبِيناً**."

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi regarding His saying, the Exalted: **لا خَيْرَ فِي كَثِيرٍ مِنْ نَجْواهُمْ** — "There is no good in much of their secret talks" (4:114) (the verse), he said: My father narrated to me from Ibn Abi Umayr from Hammad from al-Halabi from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "Indeed, Allah has enjoined *al-tamahhul* (التمحل) in the Quran." I said: "And what is *al-tamahhul*, may I be your ransom?" He said: "That your face be broader than your brother's face, so you exert effort (تمحل) for him; and that is the saying of Allah: **لا خَيْرَ فِي كَثِيرٍ مِنْ نَجْواهُمْ**."

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain to Abdullah ibn Sinan from Abu al-Jarud, who said: Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) said: "Whenever I tell you something, ask me for its basis from the Book of Allah." Then he said in part of his discourse: "Indeed, the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) forbade idle talk (القيل والقال), the wasting of wealth, and excessive questioning." It was said to him: "O son of the Messenger of Allah, where is this in the Book of Allah?" He said: "Indeed, Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, says: **لا خَيْرَ فِي كَثِيرٍ مِنْ نَجْواهُمْ إِلَّا مَنْ أَمَرَ بِصَدَقَةٍ أَوْ مَعْرُوفٍ أَوْ إِصْلاحٍ بَيْنَ النَّاسِ** — 'There is no good in much of their secret talks, except for him who enjoins charity or what is right or reconciliation between people' (4:114). And He said: **وَلا تُؤْتُوا السُّفَهاءَ أَمْوالَكُمُ الَّتِي جَعَلَ اللهُ لَكُمْ قِياماً** — 'Do not give the feeble-minded your property, which Allah has assigned to you for support' (4:5). And He said: **لا تَسْئَلُوا عَنْ أَشْياءَ إِنْ تُبْدَ لَكُمْ تَسُؤْكُمْ** — 'Do not ask about things which, if they are disclosed to you, will upset you' (5:101)."

In the *Tafsir* of al-Ayyashi, from Ibrahim ibn Abd al-Hamid from some of the reliable narrators from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him): Regarding His saying: **لا خَيْرَ فِي كَثِيرٍ مِنْ نَجْواهُمْ إِلَّا مَنْ أَمَرَ بِصَدَقَةٍ أَوْ مَعْرُوفٍ أَوْ إِصْلاحٍ بَيْنَ النَّاسِ** —he said: "By 'what is right' (المعروف) He means the loan (القرض)."

I say: Al-Qummi also narrated it in his *Tafsir* with this chain, and this meaning is narrated through Sunni chains as well. In any case, it is of the nature of application (الجري) and the mention of some instances (المصاديق).

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*: Muslim, al-Tirmidhi, al-Nasa'i, Ibn Majah, and al-Bayhaqi narrated from Sufyan ibn Abdillah al-Thaqafi who said: I said: "O Messenger of Allah, command me with a matter that I may hold fast to in Islam." He said: "Say: 'I believe in Allah,' then remain steadfast." I said: "O Messenger of Allah, what is the thing you fear most for me?" He said: "This," and the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) took hold of the tip of his own tongue.

I say: The reports censuring excessive speech and praising silence and quietude and what pertains to that are very numerous, narrated in the collections of both the Shi'a and the Sunnis.

In it: Abu Nasr al-Sijzi narrated in *al-Inabah* from Anas who said: A Bedouin came to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said to him: "O Bedouin, Allah has revealed to me in the Quran: **لا خَيْرَ فِي كَثِيرٍ مِنْ نَجْواهُمْ** — (to His saying) — **فَسَوْفَ نُؤْتِيهِ أَجْراً عَظِيماً**. O Bedouin, the 'great reward' is Paradise." The Bedouin said: "Praise be to Allah who guided us to Islam."

In it, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَمَنْ يُشاقِقِ الرَّسُولَ** (the verse): Al-Tirmidhi and al-Bayhaqi in *al-Asma' wa al-Sifat* narrated from Ibn Umar who said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "Allah will never gather this nation upon misguidance—and the hand of Allah is over the community (الجماعة). So whoever deviates, deviates into the Fire." In it: Al-Tirmidhi and al-Bayhaqi narrated from Ibn Abbas that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "Allah will never gather my nation—or he said—this nation—upon misguidance—and the hand of Allah is over the community."

I say: The narration is among the famous ones, and al-Hadi (peace be upon him) narrated it from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) in his letter to the people of Ahvaz as found in the third volume of *al-Bihar*. The discussion on the meaning of the narration was presented in the previous commentary.

In the *Tafsir* of al-Ayyashi, from Hariz from some of our companions from one of the two [Imams] (peace be upon them), who said: When the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him) was in Kufa, the people came to him and said: "Appoint for us an Imam to lead us in prayer in the month of Ramadan." He said: "No," and he forbade them from gathering for it. When evening came, they began saying: "Weep in Ramadan! O Ramadan!" Al-Harith al-A'war came to him with some people and said: "O Commander of the Faithful, the people have raised a clamor and disliked your saying." At that, he said: "Leave them to what they want; let whoever they wish lead them in prayer." He said: "So whoever **يَتَّبِعْ غَيْرَ سَبِيلِ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ نُوَلِّهِ ما تَوَلَّى وَنُصْلِهِ جَهَنَّمَ وَساءَتْ مَصِيراً** — 'follows a path other than that of the faithful, We shall abandon him to what he has turned to, and We shall cast him into hell, and it is an evil destination' (4:115)."

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*: Regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَمَنْ أَصْدَقُ مِنَ اللهِ قِيلاً** — "And who is more truthful in speech than Allah?" (4:122) (the verse): Al-Bayhaqi narrated in *al-Dala'il* from Uqbah ibn Amir—in a tradition: The departure of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) for the Battle of Tabuk, and in it—he reached Tabuk in the morning, praised Allah and lauded Him as He is worthy, then said:

"To proceed: Indeed, the most truthful of discourse is the Book of Allah; the most reliable of handholds is the word of God-consciousness (كلمة التقوى); the best of creeds is the creed of Abraham; the best of traditions is the tradition of Muhammad; the most noble of discourse is the remembrance of Allah; the best of stories is this Quran; the best of matters are those determined with resolve; the worst of matters are those newly invented; the best of guidance is the guidance of the prophets; the most noble of deaths is the killing of the martyrs; the blindest of blindness is misguidance after guidance; the best of knowledge is that which benefits; the best of guidance is that which is followed; the worst of blindness is the blindness of the heart; the upper hand is better than the lower hand; that which is little and sufficient is better than that which is much and distracting; the worst of excuses is when death arrives; the worst of regrets is on the Day of Resurrection; among the people are those who do not come to prayer except at its end; among them are those who do not remember Allah except with neglect; the greatest of sins is the lying tongue; the best of wealth is the wealth of the soul; the best of provisions is God-consciousness; the head of wisdom is the fear of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic; the best of what is settled in the hearts is certainty (اليقين); doubt is from disbelief; wailing is from the work of the Pre-Islamic period; embezzlement is from the embers of Hell; treasure is a branding from the Fire; poetry is from the flutes of Iblis; wine is the gatherer of sin; women are the snare of Satan; youth is a branch of madness; the worst of earnings is the earning of usury; the worst of food is the property of the orphan; the fortunate one is he who is admonished by others; the wretched one is he who is wretched in his mother's womb; each of you shall proceed to a place of four cubits; the matter is by its end; the criterion of a deed is its conclusion; the worst of reports are reports of lies; everything that is coming is near; reviling a believer is transgression; fighting a believer is disbelief; eating his flesh is from the disobedience of Allah; the sanctity of his property is like the sanctity of his blood; whoever swears by Allah [falsely], He will prove him a liar; whoever forgives, Allah will forgive him; whoever pardons, Allah will pardon him; whoever restrains anger, Allah will reward him; whoever is patient in calamity, Allah will compensate him; whoever seeks reputation, Allah will make him heard of; whoever is patient, Allah will double for him; and whoever disobeys Allah, Allah will punish him. O Allah, forgive me and my nation"—he said it three times—"I seek forgiveness from Allah for me and for you."

In the *Tafsir* of al-Ayyashi, from Muhammad ibn Yunus from some of his companions from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) and from Jabir from Abu Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him): Regarding the saying of Allah: **وَلَآمُرَنَّهُمْ فَلَيُغَيِّرُنَّ خَلْقَ اللهِ** — "and I will surely command them and they will surely alter Allah’s creation" (4:119)—he said: "Allah commanded what He commanded."

In it, from Jabir from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him): Regarding the saying of Allah: **وَلَآمُرَنَّهُمْ فَلَيُغَيِّرُنَّ خَلْقَ اللهِ** —he said: "The religion of Allah."

I say: The outcome of the two narrations is one, which is what was presented in the previous commentary—that it is the religion of the primordial nature (دين الفطرة).

In *al-Majma'*, regarding His saying: **فَلَيُبَتِّكُنَّ آذانَ الْأَنْعامِ** — "and they will surely slit the ears of the cattle" (4:119)—he said: "That they should cut the ears from their roots." He said: "And it is narrated from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him)."

In the *Tafsir* of al-Ayyashi regarding His saying, the Exalted: **لَيْسَ بِأَمانِيِّكُمْ** (the verse): From Muhammad ibn Muslim from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) who said: When this verse was revealed: **مَنْ يَعْمَلْ سُوءاً يُجْزَ بِهِ**, some of the companions of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "How severe a verse this is!" The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said to them: "Are you not afflicted in your wealth, your selves, and your offspring?" They said: "Yes." He said: "That is from what Allah records for you as good deeds and erases thereby the evil deeds."

I say: This meaning is narrated through many chains in the Sunni collections from the companions.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*: Ahmad, al-Bukhari, Muslim, and al-Tirmidhi narrated from Abu Sa'id al-Khudri who said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "No fatigue, nor disease, nor sorrow, nor sadness, nor hurt, nor distress befalls a believer—even the prick of a thorn—except that Allah expiates some of his sins thereby."

I say: This meaning is widely transmitted (مستفيض) from the Prophet and the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them).

In *al-Uyun*, with its chain to al-Husayn ibn Khalid from Abu al-Hasan al-Rida (peace be upon him) who said: I heard my father narrating from his father (peace be upon him) that he said: "Allah only took Abraham as a friend (خليلاً) because he never turned anyone away and never asked anyone for anything except Allah, the Mighty and Majestic."

I say: This is the most correct of the narrations regarding his (peace be upon him) being named "the friend" (الخليل) because it accords with the meaning of the word, which is "need" (الحاجة); thus, your friend (خليلك) is the one to whom you raise your needs. There are other narrated views as well.
